name: Megara
description: Megara was the eldest daughter of Creon, King of Thebes. She is primarily known for her tragic association with the hero Heracles (Hercules). Creon offered her hand in marriage to Heracles as a reward for his service in defending Thebes against the Minyans. They married and had several children, though the exact number varies in different accounts. Their life together was initially happy, but tragedy struck when Heracles was driven mad by Hera, the jealous wife of Zeus, and in his madness, he murdered Megara and their children. This act of unspeakable horror became a pivotal event in Heracles' life, leading him to seek atonement through the famous Twelve Labors. Some versions of the myth state that Heracles killed Megara and the children, while others say that Hera drove him mad and he killed them, and still others have Amphitryon, Iolaus, and Megara survive. In some accounts, Megara later remarries Heracles' nephew, Iolaus, after Heracles completes his labors, symbolizing a new beginning after the devastating loss. Megara's story is a poignant reminder of the suffering that even heroes can inflict and the devastating consequences of divine intervention in mortal lives. Her tale underscores themes of madness, loss, redemption, and the enduring power of grief in Greek mythology. Despite her tragic fate, Megara remains a significant figure, her name forever linked to the legendary Heracles and the terrible price of his heroism.
subtitle: Tragic wife of Heracles, victim of Hera.

keyMyths.title: Marriage to Heracles
keyMyths.description: Megara was given in marriage to Heracles by her father, King Creon, as a reward for his service to Thebes. This union brought a period of peace and happiness before tragedy struck.

keyMyths.title: The Madness of Heracles
keyMyths.description: Driven mad by Hera, Heracles murdered Megara and their children in a fit of rage. This act led to his self-imposed exile and the undertaking of the Twelve Labors.

keyMyths.title: Remarriage to Iolaus
keyMyths.description: In some versions, Megara remarries Iolaus, Heracles' nephew, after Heracles completes his labors. This union symbolizes a new beginning and a hope for healing after tragedy.

sources.author: Apollodorus

sources.author: Euripides
sources.description: A tragic play focusing on the madness of Heracles and the murder of his family.
